claim: UNICEF, WHO, and World Bank joint estimates report 149 million stunted children under five globally, with 40 percent in Sub-Saharan Africa where absolute numbers are rising, and stunting rates exceeding 50 percent in several countries.

source.title: Levels and Trends in Child Malnutrition - JME 2023

method: Reviewed JME report methodology, country estimates, and regional trends.
limitations: JME estimates are modeled at national and regional levels and may smooth sub-national variation.
Estimates depend on available survey data which is irregular.

claim: A 2021 Lancet series estimated that scaling ten evidence-based nutrition interventions to 90 percent coverage could reduce stunting by 20 percent, with the largest impacts from maternal nutrition, breastfeeding promotion, and complementary feeding.

source.title: Evidence-based interventions for improvement of maternal and child nutrition: what can be done and at what cost?

method: Reviewed Lancet series intervention packages, effect sizes, and coverage estimates.
limitations: Intervention coverage scaling is modeled, not observed.
Effect sizes are global averages and may not apply equally in all contexts.

claim: A 2018 Nature study mapped child stunting at 5km resolution across Africa using geostatistical methods and DHS data, finding substantial sub-national variation within countries and identifying hotspots not visible from national averages.

source.title: Mapping child growth failure in Africa between 2000 and 2015

method: Reviewed mapping methodology, spatial resolution, uncertainty quantification, and hotspot identification.
limitations: Estimates are modeled from survey data and may not reflect recent changes.
5km resolution is interpolated, not directly measured.
